title: The Agile Mindset
summary: Agile is a way of thinking and working that focuses on delivering value, learning fast, and adapting with the team and stakeholders.

sections.heading: What agile really is
sections.body:
Agile is not one method. In PMI terms, it is a mindset and approach built around iterative delivery, rapid feedback, continuous learning, and adaptation. The Agile Practice Guide emphasizes delivering value frequently, making work visible, and adjusting based on feedback instead of trying to predict everything up front.

On the PMP exam, agile answers usually favor collaboration, transparency, and incremental value delivery over heavy control and detailed early planning. In hybrid scenarios, use enough planning and governance to manage risk, but still seek feedback and adapt as the work unfolds.
sections.tip: If an exam option delivers value sooner, improves feedback, or helps the team adapt, it is often the stronger agile choice.

sections.heading: Values and principles behind the mindset
sections.body:
The Agile Manifesto values still matter for exam thinking:

Individuals and interactions over processes and tools
Working product over comprehensive documentation
Customer collaboration over contract negotiation
Responding to change over following a plan

For PMP alignment, connect those values to the PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition. Relevant principles include focusing on value, building quality into processes and deliverables, engaging stakeholders, leading with stewardship, and tailoring based on context. The exam tests application of these ideas, not memorization.
sections.tip: When two answers seem plausible, prefer the one that best supports value, stakeholder engagement, and adaptation to change.

sections.heading: How agile teams operate
sections.body:
Agile teams are typically cross-functional and self-managing. They decide how to accomplish the work, collaborate closely, and improve through regular feedback loops. The Agile Practice Guide uses 'self-managing' more broadly than older 'self-organizing' language, so either idea points to team empowerment rather than command-and-control direction.

In Scrum-based examples, the Product Owner orders the product backlog to maximize value, the Scrum Master enables the team and helps remove impediments, and developers create the increment. In broader PMP scenarios, the project manager often acts with a servant leadership style by facilitating, coaching, and supporting decision making rather than assigning every task.
sections.tip: Do not jump in to solve everything yourself. In agile scenarios, the best first move is often to enable the team to solve the problem.

sections.heading: Backlog, increments, and feedback
sections.body:
Agile work is usually managed as a prioritized backlog and delivered in small increments. The goal is to create usable outcomes, gather feedback early, and refine the product over time. The Agile Practice Guide supports using reviews, demos, retrospectives, and frequent stakeholder interaction to inspect and adapt.

Exam questions often reward actions that improve backlog clarity, stakeholder feedback, and shared understanding of value. If requirements are uncertain, the agile response is usually to clarify with stakeholders, refine backlog items, and learn through small releases rather than freeze scope too early.
sections.tip: If requirements are unclear, refine the backlog and get feedback on a small increment instead of forcing detailed up-front requirements.

sections.heading: How agile handles change
sections.body:
Agile welcomes change when it improves value, but it does not mean uncontrolled change. New ideas, defects, risks, and stakeholder requests are evaluated, added to the backlog as needed, and prioritized. The team protects the current iteration goal as much as possible, then adapts in the next planning cycle unless an urgent issue justifies immediate action.

This fits the PMP Examination Content Outline expectation to assess and manage changes, risks, and stakeholder needs using the delivery approach in play. In hybrid work, some changes may still require formal governance, so tailor the response to the project context.
sections.tip: In a sprint or iteration, avoid disruptive mid-cycle changes unless the issue is urgent. Capture, assess, and reprioritize through the backlog.

sections.heading: Ethics and mindset on the exam
sections.body:
Agile behavior on the exam should also align with the PMI Code of Ethics and Professional Conduct. That means being honest and transparent, showing respect for team members and stakeholders, taking responsibility, and acting fairly. If a scenario includes conflict, hidden information, pressure to cut corners, or blame, choose the response that is ethical, transparent, and collaborative.

Many PMP questions are not purely agile or purely predictive. The PMP Examination Content Outline reflects a mix of predictive, agile, and hybrid ways of working across People, Process, and Business Environment. First identify the environment, then choose the action that best fits that context.
sections.tip: Before answering, identify the delivery approach and eliminate any option that violates ethics, hides information, or weakens collaboration.

source: PMI sources: PMP Examination Content Outline, PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition, Agile Practice Guide, and the PMI Code of Ethics and Professional Conduct.
